Chapter: Reimbursement Process.
Current document status: Draft.

Section: Reimbursement Process.

Section: Guiding Principle.

Routine expenses within approved ministry budgets should be easy to submit and reimbursed promptly, with monthly Finance Committee review.

Expenses outside budget, over budget, unusual, recurring, or above a $TBD threshold require pre-approval before purchase whenever possible.

Section: Routine Reimbursement Process.

Use this process for ordinary ministry expenses that are within an approved budget and below the pre-approval threshold.

Section: Ministry Leader / Requester Steps.

Confirm the expense is for an approved church or ministry purpose.
Confirm the expense is within the approved ministry budget.
Make the purchase and keep the receipt or invoice.
Submit the reimbursement request within TBD days.
Include:
Requester name.
Date of purchase.
Vendor.
Amount.
Ministry, fund, or budget category.
Brief explanation of church purpose.
Receipt or invoice.
If the requester is also the ministry leader, obtain review from another authorized approver when required by policy.

Section: Bookkeeper / Financial Administrator Steps.

Receive the reimbursement request and documentation.
Confirm the request includes the required information.
Confirm a receipt or approved missing-receipt explanation is included.
Confirm the ministry, fund, or budget category is identified.
Check whether the expense appears routine, within budget, and below the pre-approval threshold.
If complete and routine, prepare the reimbursement for payment according to the approved payment process.
Record the transaction in the financial system.
Attach or file supporting documentation with the transaction record.
Add the reimbursement to the monthly Finance Committee review packet.
Flag any unclear, incomplete, unusual, outside-budget, or over-budget request for Treasurer or Finance Committee review before payment.

Section: Finance Committee Steps.

Review reimbursement activity monthly.
Confirm reimbursements are documented and coded properly.
Review any missing receipt explanations.
Review unusual, over-budget, outside-budget, recurring, or above-threshold reimbursements.
Identify policy exceptions or repeated documentation problems.
Decide whether any follow-up, policy change, ministry leader communication, or budget adjustment is needed.

Section: Expenses Requiring Pre-Approval.

Pre-approval is required before purchase whenever practical for:

Expenses outside the approved budget.
Expenses that would cause a ministry or category to exceed budget.
Expenses above $TBD.
Unusual or non-routine expenses.
Recurring subscriptions, contracts, or commitments.
Equipment, technology, furniture, or capital items above $TBD.
Travel, lodging, mileage, conference, or training expenses unless already approved.
Emergency purchases above $TBD, unless delay would create harm or prevent urgent ministry need.
Any expense that may require Deacon review, Finance Committee recommendation, or church vote.

Section: Exception Process.

Use this process for outside-budget, over-budget, unusual, recurring, emergency, or above-threshold expenses.

Requester or ministry leader identifies the need before purchase whenever possible.
Requester submits an explanation with:
Purpose.
Estimated amount.
Budget category or proposed funding source.
Reason the expense is needed.
Timing or urgency.
Finance Committee, Treasurer, Finance Chair, Pastor, or another authorized approver reviews according to the spending authority policy.
Approval or denial is documented before purchase when possible.
If approved, requester completes the purchase and submits receipt/documentation.
Bookkeeper processes the reimbursement only after approval and documentation are complete.
Finance Committee reviews the exception at the next monthly review.

Emergency exception:

If delay would create harm or prevent urgent ministry need, the authorized emergency approver may approve the purchase within policy.
The requester must submit documentation as soon as practical.
The Finance Committee should review the emergency expense at the next meeting.

Section: Missing Receipt Process.

Receipts should be required whenever possible. Missing receipts should be exceptions, not normal practice.
